concept: Magic / Wonder-working / The Breath of Becoming
description: Magic: the working by which one thing becomes another and no one can say how. The seed already holds the tree, the loved thing slowly turns real, the tear becomes a flower: 'whekelua' names the change itself when its craft is hidden, wonder's own handiwork. It is not power over anything. Phi refuses command-magic along with every other command; what this word names moves the way spring moves, unforced, unexplained, and on time.
sound_symbolism: 'whe' is the unseen breath that starts the change; the stop 'k' is the instant no one witnesses, the same hinge that turns inside 'wakomi' (surprise) and 'kelu' (become), which sits whole inside the word; the open 'lu.a' lets the becoming flow out finished. Four syllables, because what cannot be explained deserves to be said slowly.
grammatical_notes: Noun. 'whekelua wilemo shewo ka kelu.' (Magic makes the toy become real.) A domain names its own magic by standing before it: 'lopia tholu whekelua', the nursery's magic. A worker of it is 'whekelua miona', a magic person. Kin to 'kelu' (become), the change it works; 'moreluki' (transform), the change complete; and 'whemura' (dream), the mind's own unexplained making.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Impermanence wearing its festival clothes: magic is change honored as mystery rather than mastered as mechanism, met with beginner's mind.
pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: The style's whole claim: that organic form is enchantment, the lily and the vine already halfway to another world.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Every hearth culture keeps a word for the change no craft explains — the bread that rises, the spring that returns; naming it keeps wonder in the daily round.
